title: 🐾 PetClinic — Performance & Bottlenecks
description: Performance & bottleneck dashboard for the PetClinic backend: HTTP RED, HikariCP pool saturation, PostgreSQL (1M owners) scan/cache health, and JVM. Built from OpenTelemetry (agent) + postgresql receiver metrics.

panels.title: 🚦 Golden Signals — health at a glance
Throughput
panels.description: Total application request rate (req/s), excluding health-check & MCP. The workload driving every downstream cost.

panels.title: Error rate
panels.description: Share of 5xx responses. Sustained >1% means the app is failing requests — investigate before chasing latency.

panels.title: Latency p95
panels.description: 95th percentile end-to-end request latency. The experience of your slowest 1-in-20 users.

panels.title: Latency p99
panels.description: 99th percentile latency — tail latency where N+1 queries and pool contention show up first.

panels.title: DB pool — in use
panels.description: Active HikariCP connections (default max 10). Pinned near max = the pool is the bottleneck for the 1M-owner queries.

panels.title: DB pool — waiters
panels.description: Threads BLOCKED waiting for a free DB connection. Any value >0 = pool saturation → user-visible stalls. This is the #1 danger signal for this app.

panels.title: PG cache hit
panels.description: Fraction of Postgres block reads served from shared_buffers vs disk. Dropping below 99% on a 1M-row table = seq scans / missing index → disk-bound.

panels.title: Heap used
panels.description: Heap fill vs committed. Persistently high with rising post-GC live set = memory pressure / leak → GC thrash.

panels.title: Request rate by route
panels.description: Throughput split per endpoint. /api/owners (paged list over 1M rows) is the expensive one — watch its share.

panels.title: Latency percentiles (overall)
panels.description: p50/p95/p99 together. A widening gap between p50 and p99 means variance — usually lock/pool contention or GC pauses, not steady slowness.

panels.title: Latency p95 by route
panels.description: Which endpoint is slow. If /api/owners p95 climbs while others stay flat, the pagination/sort query or its indexes are the suspect.

panels.title: Errors by status code
panels.description: 4xx (client) vs 5xx (server) error rates. A spike in 5xx often coincides with pool exhaustion or DB timeouts below.

panels.title: 🔌 DB Connection Pool (HikariCP) — the classic bottleneck
Pool connections: used / idle / max
panels.description: Live pool composition. 'used' riding the 'max' ceiling with 0 idle = every request queues for a connection.

panels.title: Waiters — threads blocked on the pool
panels.description: DANGER: any sustained value >0 means requests are stalling purely because the pool is full. Fix = shorten transactions / kill N+1 / raise pool size.

panels.title: Connection acquire wait — p95
panels.description: How long a request waits to BORROW a connection. Near-zero is healthy; growth here directly adds to every endpoint's latency.

panels.title: Connection hold time — p95
panels.description: How long each request KEEPS a connection. Long holds relative to request latency hint at many round-trips per request (N+1) or slow queries.

panels.title: Cache hit ratio
panels.description: Buffer cache effectiveness. Should sit >99%. A drop = queries reading heap from disk = missing/unused index or a seq scan over the big table.

panels.title: Physical block reads/s by source  (seq-scan proxy)
panels.description: Blocks fetched from DISK (cache misses). A spike in heap_read = large sequential scans pulling the owners table off disk. Correlate with /api/owners latency.

panels.title: Index scans/s by index
panels.description: Are the indexes actually used? The V10 lower() owner indexes should light up during case-insensitive search. Flat-zero while owners latency is high = the query is NOT using them.

panels.title: Transactions/s — commits vs rollbacks
panels.description: Transaction throughput and failure. A rising rollback share signals deadlocks, constraint violations or timeouts.

panels.title: Row write ops/s
panels.description: insert / update / delete / hot_update rates. A high update rate with few HOT updates causes index bloat and more vacuum work.

panels.title: Live vs dead rows (bloat)
panels.description: Dead tuples accumulate from updates/deletes until autovacuum reclaims them. A rising dead count = table bloat → slower scans on owners.

panels.title: Relation sizes
panels.description: On-disk footprint per table and whole DB. Steep growth on the owners table forecasts when today's fast queries turn slow.

panels.title: Heap memory
panels.description: Used vs committed vs live-set-after-GC. The live set is the truth: if its floor keeps climbing across GCs, you have a real leak, not just churn.

panels.title: GC overhead & pause p95
panels.description: GC seconds spent per wall-clock second (overhead) plus p95 pause length. Overhead trending toward 0.1+ (10% of time in GC) throttles throughput.

panels.title: Threads by state
panels.description: Live threads by state. A steadily rising RUNNABLE/BLOCKED count = thread-pool growth or contention; often the flip side of pool waiters above.

panels.title: CPU utilization
panels.description: Process CPU. Pegged at 100% shifts the bottleneck from I/O to compute (serialization, mapping, GC). Cheap to overlook, expensive to ignore.

panels.title: Non-heap memory
panels.description: Metaspace / code cache. Unbounded growth here (e.g. dynamic proxies, class churn) leads to Metaspace OOM — invisible on heap charts.
